serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Sultani Dal is approximately 300 g, typically presented as a generous bowl of spiced lentil stew.

nutrient_source.title: Lentil soup, home-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 06215) adapted with butter-finish adjustment for Sultani Dal preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Sultani Dal is a Turkish red-lentil stew finished with butter, spiced with cumin, paprika, and dried mint. No exact entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA for this dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup (NDB 06215, per 100 g: energy 71 kcal, protein 4.4 g, fat 2.3 g, carbohydrate 8.3 g, fibre 1.3 g, sodium 269 mg, saturated fat 0.5 g, sugar 1.2 g) was used as the primary analogue. Values were modestly adjusted upward for fat and saturated fat to reflect the butter-finish characteristic of Sultani Dal, yielding the reported per-100 g values. Confidence is medium due to adaptation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Lentils, red, boiled (GI 32)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for Sultani Dal. Red lentils, boiled, have a well-established GI of approximately 32 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 international GI tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care). Sultani Dal is primarily a red-lentil stew; the butter and spice additions do not materially alter the GI. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 25.5) / 100 ≈ 8.2, using 300 g serving × 8.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 25.5 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Sultani Dal contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing about 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 32) and moderate dietary fibre from red lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sultani Dal has a low GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of about 8 per standard serving, making it a suitable legume-based option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter finish (~1.2 g per 100 g) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While red lentils provide beneficial dietary fibre and plant protein supportive of cardiovascular health, the butter-finish preparation adds saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size and consider requesting preparation with olive oil instead of butter.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sultani Dal is a Turkish red-lentil stew with roots in Ottoman palace cuisine, where 'Sultani' denotes a regal or richly prepared style. The dish is made by simmering red lentils with onion, garlic, tomato, cumin, and paprika until smooth and creamy, then finishing with a drizzle of browned butter (tereyağı) infused with dried mint and red pepper flakes. The result is a velvety, mildly spiced soup-stew with earthy, buttery, and subtly smoky flavours. In Singapore, it is served at Turkish and Lebanese restaurants such as Sultan Palace at Boat Quay, typically as a starter or side dish. Nutritionally, Sultani Dal is a moderate-energy dish (~78 kcal per 100 g) rich in plant protein (~4.5 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (~1.4 g per 100 g), folate, and iron. Its low glycemic index (~32) makes it appropriate for blood glucose management. The sodium content (~280 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, though not vegan due to the butter finish.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sultani Dal exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup (NDB 06215) with a modest upward adjustment for fat and saturated fat to reflect the butter-finish preparation. GI is estimated from the well-established GI of red lentils (GI 32) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to adaptation rather than exact match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy lentil soup (NDB 06215); no exact Sultani Dal entry was found in any primary regional source. Fat and saturated fat values were modestly increased to reflect the butter-finish characteristic of Sultani Dal. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): red lentils and plant-based ingredients contain no vitamin B12; the butter contribution is negligible and rounds to zero at per-100 g level — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no trans fats are present in the ingredients as reported by USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this food category. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this food category. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for this food category. 7. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on red lentils, boiled, from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Sultani Dal was found. 8. The food_group 'Meat and alternatives' is preserved from the input record as legume-based dishes are classified under this group in the source taxonomy. 9. Turkish cuisine does not require Chinese, Malay, Tamil, or other Singaporean-cuisine mandatory languages under G6; Hindi alias is included as a courtesy given the dish name's South Asian linguistic resonance. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type as no exact codes exist for this specific dish.
